title: Out-of-Distribution Detection and Selective Generation for Conditional Language Models

structured.summary: The paper presents a method for detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs in Conditional Language Models (CLMs) using embeddings from the model itself and proposes a selective generation framework. This method aims to improve the reliability of CLMs in text summarization and translation tasks by abstaining from generating outputs on low-quality OOD inputs.

strengths.point: Introduction of a lightweight OOD detection method using embeddings.
strengths.evidence: The paper proposes using input and output embeddings from CLMs to detect OOD examples, requiring negligible additional compute (Abstract and Section 2.2).
The method works in tandem with selective generation to produce higher-quality outputs on near-domain data, enabling safer deployment of CLMs (Introduction and Abstract).

weaknesses.evidence: The use of Mahalanobis distance for OOD detection is similar to previously established methods for classification, as mentioned in Section 2.2.
The paper describes the concept without providing concrete steps or an algorithm for implementation (Introduction and Section 2.2).
The paper does not present specific metrics such as precision, recall, or F1-scores to quantify the improvements claimed in the Results section.
The claim of the method being lightweight lacks empirical data or computational benchmarks to support it (Abstract and Section 2.2).
The experiments focus only on summarization and translation datasets, such as XSum and WMT15, without exploring other CLM applications (Section 3).
Specific baseline methods are mentioned but not detailed, nor are precise comparative results provided (Results section).
The method's success hinges on embedding quality, with no discussion on handling variations across different models and data (Section 2.2).
The datasets, like XSum and WMT15, do not encompass the full diversity of real-world inputs (Section 3.1).
The paper lacks a clear definition or examples distinguishing near-domain and OOD data (Section 2.2).

weaknesses.point: Inadequate explanation of background model selection.
weaknesses.evidence: The rationale for selecting specific datasets like C4 and ParaCrawl for Gaussian fitting is not thoroughly justified (Section 2.2).

questions.question: How does the method handle varying quality of embeddings across different models?
questions.motivation: Embedding quality can significantly affect the reliability of OOD detection, impacting the method's applicability across different datasets and models.

questions.question: What are the computational overheads associated with the proposed method?
questions.motivation: Understanding the computational efficiency is crucial for practical deployment, especially in resource-constrained environments.

questions.question: How generalizable is the method for other conditional language model tasks?
questions.motivation: Assessing generalizability beyond summarization and translation is important to evaluate the broader applicability of the method.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of novelty in methodology.. The use of Mahalanobis distance for OOD detection is similar to previously established methods for classification, as mentioned in Section 2.2
critique_points.point_002: Vague explanation of 'selective generation'.. The paper describes the concept without providing concrete steps or an algorithm for implementation (Introduction and Section 2.2)
critique_points.point_003: Insufficient evaluation details.. The paper does not present specific metrics such as precision, recall, or F1-scores to quantify the improvements claimed in the Results section
critique_points.point_004: Unclear impact on computational efficiency.. The claim of the method being lightweight lacks empirical data or computational benchmarks to support it (Abstract and Section 2.2)
critique_points.point_005: Limited scope of evaluation framework.. The experiments focus only on summarization and translation datasets, such as XSum and WMT15, without exploring other CLM applications (Section 3)
critique_points.point_006: Lack of clear baseline comparisons.. Specific baseline methods are mentioned but not detailed, nor are precise comparative results provided (Results section)
critique_points.point_007: Over-reliance on embedding representations.. The method's success hinges on embedding quality, with no discussion on handling variations across different models and data (Section 2.2)
critique_points.point_008: Lack of real-world data scenarios.. The datasets, like XSum and WMT15, do not encompass the full diversity of real-world inputs (Section 3.1)
critique_points.point_009: Ambiguous description of 'near-domain' data.. The paper lacks a clear definition or examples distinguishing near-domain and OOD data (Section 2.2)
critique_points.point_010: Inadequate explanation of background model selection.. The rationale for selecting specific datasets like C4 and ParaCrawl for Gaussian fitting is not thoroughly justified (Section 2.2)
